flashcards.front: What is a multi-tape Turing machine?
flashcards.back: A multi-tape Turing machine has multiple tapes, each with its own read-write head. At each step, the machine reads symbols from all tapes, and based on the current state, writes symbols on all tapes, moves each head independently, and changes state.

flashcards.front: How does a multi-tape TM simulate a single-tape TM?
flashcards.back: Given a single-tape TM configuration, simulate it on a multi-tape TM by encoding all symbols from the single tape into a single cell using delimiters (like # to separate tape sections). The simulation typically takes O(n²) steps for n input symbols.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity advantage of multi-tape TMs?
flashcards.back: Multi-tape TMs can simulate any single-tape TM but may take more time. However, for certain problems like recognizing palindromes, a 2-tape TM can solve in O(n) time while a single-tape TM requires O(n²) time.

flashcards.front: What is a multi-head Turing machine?
flashcards.back: A multi-head Turing machine has multiple (k) heads on the same tape. In one transition, the machine reads all heads simultaneously, and each head can write and move independently based on the same state.

An NTM has a transition function δ: Q × Γ → P(Q × Γ × {L,R}). At each step, from a given configuration, the machine can choose any one of multiple possible transitions, accepting if at least one computation path accepts.

flashcards.front: How does an NTM accept a language?
flashcards.back: An NTM accepts an input if there exists at least one sequence of choices (computation path) that leads from the start configuration to an accepting configuration. It rejects only if all paths reject.

flashcards.front: What is an enumeration Turing machine?
flashcards.back: An enumeration TM is a variant that enumerates (lists) all strings in a language. It outputs strings one by one, potentially running forever. A language is recursively enumerable if and only if some enumeration TM enumerates it.

flashcards.front: What is a Universal Turing Machine (UTM)?
flashcards.back: A UTM is a TM that can simulate any other TM. It takes as input: (1) encoding of the TM to be simulated, and (2) encoding of the input. It performs the computation that the encoded TM would perform on the given input.

flashcards.front: What is the Busy Beaver function Σ(n)?
flashcards.back: The Busy Beaver function Σ(n) gives the maximum number of symbols a Turing machine with n states (and 2 symbols) can write before halting, when started on a blank tape. It grows faster than any computable function.

flashcards.front: What is a computation history in TM theory?
flashcards.back: A computation history is a complete record of all configurations in a sequence from the start configuration to a halting configuration. It shows the step-by-step execution of a Turing machine on an input.

flashcards.front: How is a Turing machine encoded?
flashcards.back: A TM is encoded using a finite binary string. The encoding typically includes: number of states, alphabet symbols, transition functions (represented as tuples of state, symbol, new state, new symbol, direction). Each element is represented in binary.

flashcards.front: What is a configuration in a Turing machine?
flashcards.back: A configuration is a snapshot of the TM during computation, represented as X₁X₂...X_{i-1}qX_iX_{i+1}...X_n, where q is the current state and the head is positioned over X_i. It captures state, tape contents, and head position.

The Church-Turing thesis states that any computable function can be computed by a Turing machine. It proposes that the class of Turing-computable functions equals the class of intuitively computable (effectively calculable) functions.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between TMs and algorithms?
flashcards.back: A Turing machine formalizes the concept of an algorithm. Each algorithm can be described as a TM, and every TM describes an algorithm. This correspondence is fundamental to computability theory.

A recursive language (decidable language) is one for which there exists a Turing machine that halts on every input (accepts all strings in the language and rejects all strings not in the language). The membership problem is always resolved.
